title: How to read multiple lines of input in Python?
content: I'm having trouble reading two integers from separate lines. Can someone help?

comments.content: I had the same issue! Try using `input()` on separate lines for each integer.

replies.content: That's correct. Use `a = int(input())` on one line, then `b = int(input())` on the next. Each call to `input()` reads one line from stdin.
Thanks! That worked perfectly. I was trying to read both on the same line with `split()`.

comments.content: For competitive programming, you can also use `sys.stdin` for faster input reading, but `input()` is fine for most problems here.

title: FizzBuzz edge cases discussion
content: Let's discuss the edge cases for the FizzBuzz problem. What happens when N=1?

comments.content: When N=1, it should just print `1` since 1 is not divisible by 3 or 5.
What about N=15? That should print `FizzBuzz` right?

replies.content: Exactly! The key is checking divisibility by 15 first (or equivalently both 3 and 5), then 3, then 5. If you check 3 first you'll print `Fizz` and miss the `FizzBuzz` case.

comments.content: I got confused about the order of checks. Thanks for clarifying professor!

title: Floating point precision issues
content: My circle area calculation is off by a tiny amount. Is that expected with floating point?

comments.content: Floating point arithmetic in computers is inherently imprecise due to IEEE 754 representation. Numbers like 0.1 cannot be represented exactly in binary.

replies.content: So how does the grading system handle this?
We use a tolerance-based comparison. Your answer just needs to be within a small epsilon (e.g., 0.0001) of the expected value. So don't worry about the last few decimal places.

comments.content: I recommend using `math.pi` instead of hardcoding `3.14159`. It gives you the best precision available in your language.

replies.content: Good tip! I was indeed hardcoding it. Switched to `math.pi` and now the output matches.

title: How to format output to 2 decimal places?
content: What's the correct way to format a float to exactly 2 decimal places in Python?

comments.content: In Python, use f-strings: `print(f'{value:.2f}')`. This will always print exactly 2 decimal places, even for whole numbers.

replies.content: That works great! Thanks! I was using `round()` but it doesn't pad trailing zeros.

comments.content: You can also use `format(value, '.2f')` or the old-style `'%.2f' % value`. All three approaches produce the same result.
What about in Java? I'm using `System.out.printf("%.2f", value)` — is that correct?

replies.content: Yes, `printf` or `String.format("%.2f", value)` are both correct in Java. In C++ you'd use `cout << fixed << setprecision(2) << value`.
Perfect, thanks for the cross-language comparison!

title: Prefix sum algorithm explanation
content: Can someone explain the prefix sum approach step by step?

comments.content: The idea is to precompute cumulative sums so you can get any range sum in O(1) time instead of O(n).

replies.content: Can you give a concrete example with `[1, 2, 3, 4]`?
Sure! For `[1, 2, 3, 4]`, prefix sums are `[1, 3, 6, 10]`. Each element is the running total. To get the sum from index 1 to 3: `prefix[3] - prefix[0] = 10 - 1 = 9`.

comments.content: Great explanation! This technique is fundamental in competitive programming. You'll see it used in problems involving subarray sums, 2D matrix queries, and difference arrays.
For this specific problem, you just need to output the running sum at each step. You don't even need an array — just accumulate and print.

replies.content: Oh that simplifies things a lot. Thanks everyone!

title: Does output order matter for prime factorization?
content: The problem says order doesn't matter. Does the grading system handle that?

comments.content: Yes, the system uses **token-unordered comparison** for this problem. It splits both your output and the expected output into tokens and checks if they form the same set.

replies.content: That makes sense. So `2 3 5` and `5 3 2` would both be accepted?
Correct! As long as all the prime factors are present, the order doesn't matter.

comments.content: Just make sure to separate factors with spaces and don't include duplicate factors. For example, `12 = 2 * 2 * 3` should output `2 3`, not `2 2 3`.

replies.content: Wait, is it distinct prime factors or all prime factors? The problem says distinct right?
Yes, the problem asks for **distinct** prime factors only. So for 12, output `2 3`.

title: String reversal approaches
content: What are the different ways to reverse a string in Python?

comments.content: The easiest and most Pythonic way is slicing: `s[::-1]`. It creates a reversed copy in one expression.
You can also use `''.join(reversed(s))`. The `reversed()` function returns an iterator, so you need to join it back into a string.

replies.content: Nice! I didn't know about the `reversed()` function. Which one is faster?
Slicing `[::-1]` is generally faster because it's implemented in C under the hood. `reversed()` + `join()` has more Python overhead. For this problem either works fine though.

comments.content: In Java or C++, you'd typically use a `StringBuilder.reverse()` or `std::reverse()`. For a manual approach, you can swap characters from both ends moving inward.

replies.content: Good to know for when we switch languages. Thanks for all the tips!
